EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front spawned a shallow line of convection that produced severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 50 knots (58 MPH) was measured by a mesonet in SW DC. A tree blew down onto a bike rack and bus stop structure near the DC General Health Campus.

A tree blew down onto a house in the 3000 block of Brandywine Street Northwest. A tree blew down in the 4000 block of Connecticut Avenue Northwest. A tree blew down onto Yuma Street Northwest in Tenleytown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blew down onto a house on Walter Street Southeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sparked scattered severe thunderstorms that produced mainly damaging straight-line wind gusts across much of Virginia into parts of Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blew down near the intersection of Klingle Road Northwest and Adams Mill Road Northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sparked scattered severe thunderstorms that produced mainly damaging straight-line wind gusts across much of Virginia into parts of Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blew down in the 500 block of 21st Street Northeast.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blew down in the 5000 block of Call Place Southeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough and approaching cold front ignited multiple rounds of severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. Some thunderstorms produced significant microbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were about two dozen calls for trees and wires down throughout Northwest DC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through the Ohio Valley. Warmer air aloft overran colder air in place that was associated with high pressure over New England, resulting in freezing rain. Ice accumulations were less than a tenth of an inch before precipitation changed to rain.

Closed low pressure slowly tracked into the Ohio Valley on the 31st before transferring its energy to low pressure off the Mid-Atlantic Coast on the 1st of February. The coastal low slowly tracked northeast while intensifying through midday on February the 2nd. ||Snow overspread the area on the 31st of January ahead of the first low. Drier air and warmer air moved in aloft, causing snow to change to a light wintry mix on the night of the 31st into the 1st. However, another period of snow developed as the coastal low strengthened to the east later on the 1st and that continued into the 2nd. ||Total snowfall accumulations were around 3 to 5 inches and ice accumulation was around one-tenth of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wintry precipitation continued at times into February 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked into the Ohio Valley on the 13th before transferring its energy to a coastal low that moved offshore on the 14th. A period of sleet and freezing rain occurred across the area. Ice accumulations were around one-quarter inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell onto powerlines in the 3000 Block of Ordway Street Northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough and approaching cold front ignited multiple rounds of severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. Some thunderstorms produced significant microbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree blew down on Canal Road Northwest near Arizone Avenue Northwest. Multiple other trees were blown down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several showers and thunderstorms in a strong low-level wind shear environment produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees and branches blew down near Rock Creek Park blocking Beach Drive Northwest for several hours; several large branches also blew down nearby in the 4000 block of Calvert Street Northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms produced two tornadoes, hail, and widespread wind damage (some significant) across the DC metro and central Maryland, with spottier wind damage extending into the Baltimore area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Canal Road Northwest was blocked by fallen trees and wires between Arizona Avenue Northwest and Foxhall Road Northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms produced two tornadoes, hail, and widespread wind damage (some significant) across the DC metro and central Maryland, with spottier wind damage extending into the Baltimore area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado that began in Arlington crossed the Potomac River near the Theodore Roosevelt Memorial Bridge on I-66/US-50. The first sign of tornadic damage in the District of Columbia was at the west end of the National Mall, with a snapped hardwood tree on The Mall on 23rd Street Northwest between US-50 Constitution Avenue Northwest and Lincoln Memorial Circle. Along US-50 Constitution Avenue Northwest, wind damage to trees paralleling the roadway was prominent from 23rd Street Northwest east for about three quarters of a mile to near 16th Street Northwest south of The Ellipse. Tree limbs fell to the north on US-50 Constitution Avenue Northwest. Concentrated damage at the north portion of Constitution Gardens was chaotic, with hardwood limbs and downed trees falling in multiple directions. The last discernible tornadic damage was to temporary fencing on The Mall that was installed for the Independence Day celebration, directly across from The Ellipse. This fencing appeared to have been lifted up and twisted, landing in a mangled and haphazard manner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms produced two tornadoes, hail, and widespread wind damage (some significant) across the DC metro and central Maryland, with spottier wind damage extending into the Baltimore area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado separate from the one that impacted the National Mall touched down in the H Street Corridor at 9:08 PM EDT. A large tree limb fell on two cars along the 600 block of I Street Northeast. A large hardwood tree was snapped along the 900 block of 7th Street Northeast and fell due north. Numerous smaller tree limbs were downed in this area.||The tornado proceeded east along the H Street corridor, crossing over Maryland Avenue Northeast causing intermittent downed tree limbs. Several hardwood trees were down near the Rosedale Recreation Center property, falling due west. The circulation then diminished at 9:10 PM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms produced two tornadoes, hail, and widespread wind damage (some significant) across the DC metro and central Maryland, with spottier wind damage extending into the Baltimore area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blew down on 14th Street Northeast near Monroe Street Northeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms produced two tornadoes, hail, and widespread wind damage (some significant) across the DC metro and central Maryland, with spottier wind damage extending into the Baltimore area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and large branches blew down along the south end of the National Mall to near the Thomas Jefferson Memorial.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several thunderstorms ahead of a cold front produced widely scattered instances of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blew down onto a car in the 4700 block of First Street Southwest, briefly trapping five occupants. Other large branches were blown down and awning damage was reported in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms produced two tornadoes, hail, and widespread wind damage (some significant) across the DC metro and central Maryland, with spottier wind damage extending into the Baltimore area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were a few dozen calls for trees blown down in DC. Five people were injured when a five story dwelling under construction collapsed in the 900 block of Kennedy Street Northwest; one person was trapped and later freed. A large tree blew down onto eastbound Massachusetts Avenue Northeast near 10th Street Northeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly flow allowed for plenty of moisture ahead of a cold front. The moisture along with an unstable atmosphere caused heavy rain to be associated with thunderstorms, and this led to instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All lanes of Nannie Helen Burroughs Avenue Northeast were blocked by high water at the DC-295 Anacostia Beltway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly flow allowed for plenty of moisture ahead of a cold front. The moisture along with an unstable atmosphere caused heavy rain to be associated with thunderstorms, and this led to instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All lanes of East Capitol Street were blocked by flooding at the DC-295 Anacostia Freeway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly flow allowed for plenty of moisture ahead of a cold front. The moisture along with an unstable atmosphere caused heavy rain to be associated with thunderstorms, and this led to instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All lanes of Eastern Avenue Northeast were blocked by flooding near Minnesota Avenue Northeast and Addison Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled out over the area with tropical moisture in place. Thunderstorms caused heavy rain that led to instances of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All lanes were closed on  East Capitol Street under the 295 overpass due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled out over the area with tropical moisture in place. Thunderstorms caused heavy rain that led to instances of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle was submerged along southbound 295 at the Benning Road underpass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled out over the area with tropical moisture in place. Thunderstorms caused heavy rain that led to instances of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread street flooding to depths up to a couple of feet occurred along Anacostia Avenue and Cassell Place Northeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of fronts spawned two clusters of severe thunderstorms that produced localized considerable wind damage near Staunton, Virginia, Silver Spring, Maryland, and Washington, DC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were over 100 trees, branches and wires blown down throughout DC. One of the hardest hit areas was in Northwest DC, where several dozen trees, limbs and wires blew down in the Petworth, Columbia Heights and Mount Pleasant neighborhoods; minor roof damage was observed at at least two apartment buildings in Petworth. The railing of a rooftop of one apartment building was torn off, with a large tree falling and crushing cars nearby. A section of parapet wall and roof decking blew off a second (vacant) apartment building in the 2900 block of Ontario Road Northwest. A large tree blew over (split at the trunk) and fell on and crushed a car near the intersection of Taylor Street Northwest and Kansas Avenue Northwest. A tree blew down onto Georgia Avenue Northwest near Taylor Street Northwest. Several 3 to 6 inch in diameter tree limbs blew down on 14th Street Northwest near Arkansas Avenue Northwest. A tree blew down in the 1200 block of Randolph Street Northwest.  A large tree blew down blocking portions of Quincy Street Northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of fronts spawned two clusters of severe thunderstorms that produced localized considerable wind damage near Staunton, Virginia, Silver Spring, Maryland, and Washington, DC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were over 100 trees, branches and wires blown down throughout DC, including in the Adams Morgan, Shaw, and Edgewood neighborhoods in Northwest DC, where a few dozen trees, branches and wires blew down. A wind gust of 58 knots (67 MPH) was measured by a mesonet at Children's Hospital. A tree blew down near the intersection of 15th Street Northwest and S Street Northwest. Several large tree limbs blew down on 16th Street Northwest near Kalorama Road Northwest. A very large tree blew down onto a row house in the 600 block of Irving Street Northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of fronts spawned two clusters of severe thunderstorms that produced localized considerable wind damage near Staunton, Virginia, Silver Spring, Maryland, and Washington, DC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were over 100 trees, branches and wires blown down throughout DC. Several of them were in the Queens Chapel, Avondale, University Heights and Brookland neighborhoods in Northeast DC, including a tree that blew down onto a house in the 1300 block of Webster Street Northeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of fronts spawned two clusters of severe thunderstorms that produced localized considerable wind damage near Staunton, Virginia, Silver Spring, Maryland, and Washington, DC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were over 100 trees, branches and wires blown down throughout DC. Many of them  blew down in the Noma and Brentwood neighborhoods of Northeast DC, including a tree that blew down onto an occupied vehicle in the 1100 block of 5th Street Northeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure and southwesterly flow allowed heat and humidity to build across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices rose to between 105 and 110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary remain draped overhead while a deep southerly flow ushered in plenty of moisture. Showers and thunderstorms resulted in heavy rainfall that led to an instance of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broad Branch Road NW was closed both ways around Brandywine Street NW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon, leading to isolated instances of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park Rd. closed due to flooding near intersection with Beach Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms produced considerable wind damage over Northwest DC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees blew down on 16th Street Northwest near Piney Branch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak boundary tapped into plenty of moisture and instability for showers and thunderstorms. Weak winds aloft caused slow moving storms, which led to instance of flooding and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dozens of reports of roads closed were received in the District of Columbia due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak boundary tapped into plenty of moisture and instability for showers and thunderstorms. Weak winds aloft caused slow moving storms, which led to instance of flooding and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dozens of reports of roads closed were received in the District of Columbia due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms produced considerable wind damage over Northwest DC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees blew down on northbound US-29 Georgia Avenue Northwest near Jefferson Street Northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms produced a few downbursts from Northeast DC into nearby adjacent metro Maryland and over southeastern Anne Arundel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blew down in the 3000 block of Walnut Street Northeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front intersected an extremely unstable airmass. The result was numerous strong to severe thunderstorms containing damaging wind gusts and some hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were a couple of calls for trees down in DC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak boundary tapped into plenty of moisture and instability for showers and thunderstorms. Weak winds aloft caused slow moving storms, which led to instance of flooding and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was over a foot of water flowing at the intersection of 19th Street NE and D Street NE. Manhole covers were removed from the street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms produced considerable wind damage over Northwest DC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were about two dozen reports of trees and wires blown down across DC, mainly in Northwest. A few large oak trees up to a few feet in diameter blew down onto three houses in the 5100 block of Fordham Road Northwest. A tree blew down on Canal Road Northwest near Arizona Avenue Northwest. A tree blew down on Dalecarlia Parkway Northwest between Massachusetts Avenue Northwest and Loughboro Road Northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee pressure trough ignited thunderstorms in a strongly unstable environment. The strongest thunderstorms produced severe downbursts and sporadic hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 call center received nearly two dozen calls for scattered tree, branch and power line damage throughout DC, including along Canal Road Northwest near Fletcher's Boathouse, and on 14th Street Northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between low pressure over the Ohio Valley and strong high pressure over Quebec, CA resulted in a strong southeast onshore flow that brought the highest water levels to the Tidal Potomac River since Tropical Storm Isabel in September 2003.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate coastal flooding was observed for three consecutive high tide cycles with DC Waterfront reaching major flood stage at 224 PM LST on the 29th of October having reached a crest of 7.12 ft MLLW. Peak inundation levels were around 4 ft. Significant coastal flooding was observed in the area of the Tidal Basin and the Fish Market.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front approached the area on the 22nd before passing through on the 23rd. A deep southerly flow allowed for tropical moisture to move into the area ahead of the boundary. This led to heavy rainfall as the cold front approached, and several instances of flooding along with isolated instances of flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ramp from the 7th Street Southwest to eastbound I-395 Dwight D. Eisenhower Southwest Freeway was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A light flow aloft along with an unstable atmosphere led to slow moving showers and thunderstorms. Heavy amounts of rain led to instances of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maine Avenue SW Westbound at Independence Avenue closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A light flow aloft along with an unstable atmosphere led to slow moving showers and thunderstorms. Heavy amounts of rain led to instances of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rock Creek and Potomac Parkway NW closed near K Street NW due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Ida produced instances of flooding and flash flooding across the area. Tropical moisture infiltrated the area and there were moderate amounts of instability as well. This combined with the lift provided from the remnants of Ida led to heavy rainfall during this time. Rainfall amounts averaged around 1-3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broad Branch Drive Northwest was washed out between Davenport Street Northwest and Ridge Road Northwest. Trees and boulders were floating across where the roadway used to be.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Ida produced instances of flooding and flash flooding across the area. Tropical moisture infiltrated the area and there were moderate amounts of instability as well. This combined with the lift provided from the remnants of Ida led to heavy rainfall during this time. Rainfall amounts averaged around 1-3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The water level along the Rock Creek in Washington DC rose above flood stage of 7 feet, cresting at 8.04 feet at 11:45 AM EST on the 1st of September. Water reached several portions of the Valley Trail between Picnic Areas 7 and 10 in Rock Creek Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front approached the area on the 22nd before passing through on the 23rd. A deep southerly flow allowed for tropical moisture to move into the area ahead of the boundary. This led to heavy rainfall as the cold front approached, and several instances of flooding along with isolated instances of flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The water level along the Rock Creek in Washington DC rose above flood stage of 7 feet, cresting at 7.33 feet at 7:45 AM EST on the 23rd of September. Water reached several portions of the Valley Trail between Picnic Areas 7 and 10 in Rock Creek Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between low pressure over the Ohio Valley and strong high pressure over Quebec, CA resulted in a strong southeast onshore flow that brought the highest water levels to the Tidal Potomac River since Tropical Storm Isabel in September 2003.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Potomac River tide gauge at Wisconsin Ave reached moderate coastal flooding at 1032 AM LST on Oct 29th with a crest of 8.19 ft at 224 PM LST.
